respondent:
Various Stakeholders at Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada
(IRCC)
description:
AAST is a processing aid that supports staff in completing administrative
tasks for immigration applications, including data-matching and bulk email
creation. Two of the modules are in scope of the Treasury Board Directive on
Automated Decision Making.
The modules in question, the Biometric and Medical modules, use index
matching of lists of biographical information between the biometric
enrolments and immgration medical examinations, and applications pending
those assessments. The processing aid does not review the results of the
applicant's immigration medical examinations or biometric enrollment, and
only compares basic biographical data to score a level of match. This allows
staff to focus on non-routine suggested matches. Once the lists are
generated, the recommended information is entered into the Global Case
Management System (GCMS), which is IRCC's system of record. This helps
ensure that biometric enrollments and medical assessments are connected to
immigration applications in a timely manner.
AAST uses natural GCMS data points in processing. AAST does not interact
with GCMS or Web browsers and it does not contain a black box algorithm.
AAST does not make a decision on behalf of an employee, it makes a
recommendation on matching assessments to applications. The
assessments must then be entered into GCMS, and they continue to be
reviewed by an immigration officer prior to a decision being rendered on the
application.
qa_section_text:
Section 3.1: Impact Questions and Answers
Reasons for Automation
1. What is motivating your team to introduce automation into this decision-making process?
(Check all that apply)
The system is performing tasks that humans could not accomplish in a
reasonable period of time
Use innovative approaches
Other (please specify)
2. Please describe
Facilitate more efficient use of IRCC resources in application processing
through economies of scale, assisting in managing the growing volume of
applications, and allowing staff to focus on more complex tasks such as
outstanding assessments.
3. What client needs will the system address and how will this system meet them? If possible,
describe how client needs have been identified.
The two relevant AAST functionalities allow for faster and more efficient
completion of administrative tasks of recommending matches of biometric
enrollments and completed medical examinations to the correct application,
which ultimately reduces repeated requests to applicants and ensures
smoother processing of their application.
4. Please describe any public benefits the system is expected to have.
With more efficient completion of administrative tasks, AAST is expected to
lead to a realization of economies of scale. Additionally, since the tool can
locate and recommend matches for unmatched biometric enrolments and
immigration medical examinations, it reduces potential repeat requests to
applicants and improves client service.
5. How effective will the system likely be in meeting client needs?
Moderately effective [ Points: +1 ]
6. Please describe any improvements, benefits, or advantages you expect from using an
automated system. This could include relevant program indicators and performance targets.
• Re-allocation of resources to focus on administrative tasks that require more
in depth review such as outstanding assessments.
• Streamlining process
• Improved client service by reducing repeat requests
• Economies of scale
7. Please describe how you will ensure that the system is confined to addressing the client
needs identified above.
Functionality is limited to support administrative activities with no capacity for
or interface with other stages of application processing.
8. Please describe any trade-offs between client interests and program objectives that you have
considered during the design of the project.
There are no trade-offs. AAST is designed to fulfill both the client interests and
the program objectives.
9. Have alternative non-automated processes been considered?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
10. If non-automated processes were considered, why was automation identified as the
preferred option?
The automated process allows for streamlining and review of a higher volume
in a reduced amount of time, as opposed to a more time consuming and
labour intensive manual approach.
11. What would be the consequence of not deploying the system?
Service cannot be delivered in a timely or efficient manner [ Points: +2 ]
Service delivery cannot achieve performance targets [ Points: 0 ]
Service quality is not as high [ Points: 0 ]
Risk Profile
12. Is the project within an area of intense public scrutiny (e.g. because of privacy concerns)
and/or frequent litigation?
No [ Points: +0 ]
13. Are clients in this line of business particularly vulnerable?
No [ Points: +0 ]
14. Are stakes of the decisions very high?
No [ Points: +0 ]
15. Will this project have major impacts on staff, either in terms of their numbers or their roles?
No [ Points: +0 ]
16. Will the use of the system create or exacerbate barriers for persons with disabilities?
No [ Points: +0 ]
Project Authority
17. Will you require new policy authority for this project?
No [ Points: +0 ]
About the Algorithm
18. The algorithm used will be a (trade) secret
No [ Points: +0 ]
19. The algorithmic process will be difficult to interpret or to explain
No [ Points: +0 ]
About the Decision
20. Please describe the decision(s) that will be automated.
* Medical module - By using index matching of biographical information, this
functionality is able to recommend completed immigration medical
examinations that can be associated to specific applications in GCMS. The
functionality does not review the results of the immigration medical
examination, and only compares basic applicant biographical data to score
level of match for perfect and partial matches. Perfect matches can be
associated with minimal review, allowing staff to focus on non-routine or
partial suggested matches. AAST does not make a decision on behalf of an
employee, it makes a recommendation on matching the immigration medical
examinations to applications. The assessments continue to be reviewed by an
immigration officer prior to a decision being rendered on the application.
* Biometrics module - By using index matching of biographical information,
this functionality is able to recommend biometric enrolments that can be

_full_text:
Algorithmic Impact Assessment Results
Version: 0.10.0
Project Details
1. Name of Respondent
Various Stakeholders at Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada
(IRCC)
2. Job Title
Various Stakeholders
3. Department
Citizenship and Immigration (Department of)
4. Branch
International Platform Branch
5. Project Title
Administrative Activities Support Tool (AAST)
6. Departmental Program (from Department Results Framework)
Visitors, International students and Temporary workers and Immigrant and
Refugee Selection and Integration
7. Project Phase
Implementation [ Points: 0 ]
8. Please provide a project description:
AAST is a processing aid that supports staff in completing administrative
tasks for immigration applications, including data-matching and bulk email
creation. Two of the modules are in scope of the Treasury Board Directive on
Automated Decision Making.
The modules in question, the Biometric and Medical modules, use index
matching of lists of biographical information between the biometric
enrolments and immgration medical examinations, and applications pending
those assessments. The processing aid does not review the results of the
applicant's immigration medical examinations or biometric enrollment, and
only compares basic biographical data to score a level of match. This allows
staff to focus on non-routine suggested matches. Once the lists are
generated, the recommended information is entered into the Global Case
Management System (GCMS), which is IRCC's system of record. This helps
ensure that biometric enrollments and medical assessments are connected to
immigration applications in a timely manner.
AAST uses natural GCMS data points in processing. AAST does not interact
with GCMS or Web browsers and it does not contain a black box algorithm.
AAST does not make a decision on behalf of an employee, it makes a
recommendation on matching assessments to applications. The
assessments must then be entered into GCMS, and they continue to be
reviewed by an immigration officer prior to a decision being rendered on the
application.
About The System
9. Please check which of the following capabilities apply to your system.
Content generation: Analyzing large data sets to categorize, process, triage,
personalize, and serve specific content for specific contexts
Process optimization and workflow automation: Analyzing large data sets to
identify and anomalies, cluster patterns, predict outcomes or ways to
optimize; and automate specific workflows
Section 1: Impact Level : 1
Current Score: 27
Raw Impact Score: 27
Mitigation Score: 25
Section 2: Requirements Specific to Impact Level 1
Peer review
None
Gender-based Analysis Plus
None
Notice
None
Human-in-the-loop for decisions
Decisions may be rendered without direct human involvement.
Explanation
In addition to any applicable legal requirement, ensure that a meaningful explanation is published
for common decision results. The explanation must provide a general description of:
the role of the system in the decision-making process;
input data, its source and method of collection;
the criteria used to evaluate input data and the operations applied to process it;
the output produced by the system and any relevant information needed to interpret it in the
context of the administrative decision; and
the principal factors behind a decision.
Explanations must also inform clients of relevant recourse options, where appropriate.
Descriptions must be made available in plain language through the Algorithmic Impact
Assessment and discoverable via a departmental website.
Training
None
IT and business continuity management
None
Approval for the system to operate
None
Other requirements
The Directive on Automated Decision-Making also includes other requirements that must be met
for all impact levels.
Link to the Directive on Automated Decision-Making
Contact your institution's ATIP office to discuss the requirement for a Privacy Impact
Assessment as per the Directive on Privacy Impact Assessment.
Section 3: Questions and Answers
Section 3.1: Impact Questions and Answers
Reasons for Automation

(Check all that apply)
The system is performing tasks that humans could not accomplish in a
reasonable period of time
Use innovative approaches
Other (please specify)
2. Please describe
Facilitate more efficient use of IRCC resources in application processing
through economies of scale, assisting in managing the growing volume of
applications, and allowing staff to focus on more complex tasks such as
outstanding assessments.
3. What client needs will the system address and how will this system meet them? If possible,
describe how client needs have been identified.
The two relevant AAST functionalities allow for faster and more efficient
completion of administrative tasks of recommending matches of biometric
enrollments and completed medical examinations to the correct application,
which ultimately reduces repeated requests to applicants and ensures
smoother processing of their application.
4. Please describe any public benefits the system is expected to have.
With more efficient completion of administrative tasks, AAST is expected to
lead to a realization of economies of scale. Additionally, since the tool can
locate and recommend matches for unmatched biometric enrolments and
immigration medical examinations, it reduces potential repeat requests to
applicants and improves client service.

Functionality is limited to support administrative activities with no capacity for

There are no trade-offs. AAST is designed to fulfill both the client interests and

9. Have alternative non-automated processes been considered?

The automated process allows for streamlining and review of a higher volume
in a reduced amount of time, as opposed to a more time consuming and
labour intensive manual approach.

Project Authority

About the Algorithm
18. The algorithm used will be a (trade) secret
No [ Points: +0 ]
19. The algorithmic process will be difficult to interpret or to explain
No [ Points: +0 ]
About the Decision
20. Please describe the decision(s) that will be automated.
* Medical module - By using index matching of biographical information, this
functionality is able to recommend completed immigration medical
examinations that can be associated to specific applications in GCMS. The
functionality does not review the results of the immigration medical
examination, and only compares basic applicant biographical data to score
level of match for perfect and partial matches. Perfect matches can be
associated with minimal review, allowing staff to focus on non-routine or
partial suggested matches. AAST does not make a decision on behalf of an
employee, it makes a recommendation on matching the immigration medical
examinations to applications. The assessments continue to be reviewed by an
immigration officer prior to a decision being rendered on the application.
* Biometrics module - By using index matching of biographical information,
this functionality is able to recommend biometric enrolments that can be
associated to applications. The functionality does not review the results of
the biometric enrolment, it only compares basic applicant biographical data to
score a level of match for perfect and partial matches. Perfect matches can
be associated with minimal review, allowing staff to focus on non-routine or
partial suggested matches. AAST does not make a decision on behalf of an
employee, it makes a recommendation on matching unassociated biometric
enrolments to applications. The assessments continue to be reviewed by an
immigration officer prior to a decision being rendered on the application.
21. Does the decision pertain to any of the categories below (check all that apply):
Other (please specify) [ Points: +1 ]
22. Please describe
Immigration Services
Impact Assessment
23. Which of the following best describes the type of automation you are planning?
Partial automation (the system will contribute to administrative decision-
making by supporting an officer through assessments, recommendations,
intermediate decisions, or other outputs) [ Points: +2 ]
24. Please describe the role of the system in the decision-making process.
The Medical and Biometric modules include an automated recommendation
of matches between applications and unassociated biometric enrolments and
immigration medical examinations. AAST uses applicant biographic details
from GCMS (open applications with pending immigration medical
examinations or biometric enrollments, and unassociated immigration
medical examinations and biometric enrollments). It compares the biographic
data points to rank unassociated immigration medical examinations and
biometric enrolments with the open applications based on level of match.
Once the recommendations have been entered into GCMS applications, they
are reviewed by an immigration officer prior to final decision.
25. Will the system be making decisions or assessments that require judgement or discretion?
No [ Points: +0 ]
26. Please describe the criteria used to evaluate client data and the operations applied to
process it.
Criteria: Biographic information of applicant, biographic information of
completed and unassociated immigration medical examinations (medical
module), biographic information of completed and unassociated biometric
enrolment (biometric module).
Operations: string comparison in Global Case Management System
immigration data - specifically immigration applications and data on
immigration medical examinations (medical module) or biometric enrolments
in holding tank (i.e. not yet associated to an application) (biometric module).
Operations: string comparison between the Global Case Management System
Immigration Data and the Immigration Medical Examination data / Enterprise
Data Warehouse biometric holding tank.
27. Please describe the output produced by the system and any relevant information needed to
interpret it in the context of the administrative decision.
The output for each module is three spreadsheets listing applicants'
biographic information in their GCMS application and in their immigration
medical examination (medical module) or biometrics enrollment (biometric
module) side-by-side. Administrative staff can, based on the information
presented in the tables, determine whether the result is a match or requires
further review. These are the same pieces of information the staff would
compare in GCMS if performing the task without the processing aid; AAST
organizes the information and presents potential matches.
28. Will the system perform an assessment or other operation that would not otherwise be
completed by a human?
No [ Points: +0 ]
29. Is the system used by a different part of the organization than the ones who developed it?
No [ Points: +0 ]
30. Are the impacts resulting from the decision reversible?
Reversible [ Points: +1 ]
31. How long will impacts from the decision last?
Some impacts may last a matter of months, but some lingering impacts may
last longer [ Points: +2 ]
32. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
AAST does not make an immigration decision on behalf of an employee, it
makes a recommendation on matching assessments to applications, and the
assessments continue to be reviewed by an immigration officer prior to a
decision being rendered on the application. AAST does not impact the content
or results of the assessments themselves.
33. The impacts that the decision will have on the rights or freedoms of individuals will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
34. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The functionalities will not have an impact on the rights or freedoms of
individuals. AAST does not make an immigration decision on behalf of an
employee, it makes a recommendation on matching assessments to
applications, and the assessments continue to be reviewed by an immigration
officer prior to a decision being rendered on the application. AAST does not
impact the content or results of the assessments themselves.
35. The impacts that the decision will have on the equality, dignity, privacy, and autonomy of
individuals will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
36. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The functionalities will not have an impact on the equality, dignity, privacy and
autonomy of individuals AAST does not make an immigration decision on
behalf of an employee, it makes a recommendation on matching
assessments to applications, and the assessments continue to be reviewed
by an immigration officer prior to a decision being rendered on the application.
AAST does not impact the content or results of the assessments themselves.
All information is being used for the processing of the immigration application
in accordance with the Privacy Act, and safeguards are in place to ensure
protection of personal information.
37. The impacts that the decision will have on the health and well-being of individuals will likely
be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
38. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The functionalities will not have an impact on the health and well-being of
individuals. AAST is used only to aid the processing of immigration
applications, and does not make an immigration decision on behalf of an
immigration officer.
39. The impacts that the decision will have on the economic interests of individuals will likely
be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
40. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The modules will not have an impact on the economic interests of individuals.
AAST is used only to aid the processing of immigration applications, and does
not make an immigration decision on behalf of an immigration officer.
41. The impacts that the decision will have on the ongoing sustainability of an environmental
ecosystem, will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
42. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The modules will not have an impact on the ongoing sustainability of the
environmental ecosystem as AAST is used only to aid the processing of
immigration applications.
About the Data - A. Data Source
43. Will the Automated Decision System use personal information as input data?
Yes [ Points: +4 ]
44. Have you verified that the use of personal information is limited to only what is directly
related to delivering a program or service?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
45. Is the personal information of individuals being used in a decision-making process that
directly affects those individuals?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
46. Have you verified if the system is using personal information in a way that is consistent
with: (a) the current Personal Information Banks (PIBs) and Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs)
of your programs or (b) planned or implemented modifications to the PIBs or PIAs that take new
uses and processes into account?
No [ Points: +1 ]
47. What is the highest security classification of the input data used by the system? (Select one)
Protected B / Protected C [ Points: +3 ]
48. Who controls the data?
Federal government [ Points: +1 ]
49. Will the system use data from multiple different sources?
No [ Points: +0 ]
50. Will the system require input data from an Internet- or telephony-connected device? (e.g.
Internet of Things, sensor)
No [ Points: +0 ]
51. Will the system interface with other IT systems?
No [ Points: +0 ]
52. Who collected the data used for training the system?
Your institution [ Points: +1 ]
53. Who collected the input data used by the system?
Your institution [ Points: +1 ]
54. Please describe the input data collected and used by the system, its source, and method of
collection.
* Biometric enrolments are completed by Visa Application Centres (VACs) and
IRCC offices. The client is photographed and fingerprinted using Government
of Canada software and hardware. That information is then transmitted to
GCMS, where basic biographic details of the client is exported and entered
into the Biometric Module in AAST.
* Immigration medical examinations are conducted by Panel Physicians who
submit the information electronically to IRCC. That information is then
transmitted to GCMS, where basic biographic details of the client are exported
and entered into the Medical Module in AAST.
About the Data - B. Type of Data
55. Will the system require the analysis of unstructured data to render a recommendation or a
decision?
No [ Points: 0 ]
